findings.title: Dangerous import: IoCreateDevice
findings.description: Creates device object — check if SecurityDescriptor is NULL

findings.title: Dangerous import: EtwUnregister
findings.description: Unregisters ETW provider

findings.title: Dangerous import: ExAllocatePoolWithTag
findings.description: Pool allocation — check for size validation

findings.title: WDF (Windows Driver Framework) driver detected
findings.description: Driver uses WDF framework (detected imports: WdfVersionBind). IOCTL dispatch uses EvtIoDeviceControl callback via WdfIoQueueCreate rather than DriverObject->MajorFunction[].

findings.title: Hardcoded address: 0x2080000
findings.description: Hardcoded page-aligned address found. May be a physical memory address used with MmMapIoSpace or similar.

findings.title: Missing mitigation: Signature enforcement (FORCE_INTEGRITY)
findings.description: Signature enforcement (FORCE_INTEGRITY) is not enabled. Driver can load without a valid Authenticode signature.

findings.title: Driver is NOT HVCI-compatible
findings.description: Driver fails HVCI checks (missing FORCE_INTEGRITY or has W+X sections). Cannot load in HVCI-enabled environments without policy bypass.

findings.title: Kernel write gadgets found (8 instances)
findings.description: Multiple 'mov [reg+offset], reg' patterns in driver code. If the register is derived from user input (e.g., IOCTL buffer), this is an arbitrary kernel memory write primitive.

details.gadgets: 0x140001000: mov qword ptr [rsp + 8], rbx; 0x140001013: mov qword ptr [rcx], rax; 0x140001050: mov qword ptr [rsp + 8], rbx; 0x1400010A0: mov qword ptr [rsp + 0x10], rbx

findings.title: Hidden function with purpose 'etw disable' at 0x140001696
findings.description: Function at 0x140001696 performs 'etw disable' but is not reachable via the normal DriverEntry → MajorFunction call graph.  It may be invoked via a timer, DPC, work item, or kernel callback — a hidden secondary attack path.

findings.title: Hidden function with purpose 'etw register' at 0x14000170C
findings.description: Function at 0x14000170C performs 'etw register' but is not reachable via the normal DriverEntry → MajorFunction call graph.  It may be invoked via a timer, DPC, work item, or kernel callback — a hidden secondary attack path.

findings.title: Hidden function with purpose 'etw register' at 0x140001934
findings.description: Function at 0x140001934 performs 'etw register' but is not reachable via the normal DriverEntry → MajorFunction call graph.  It may be invoked via a timer, DPC, work item, or kernel callback — a hidden secondary attack path.

findings.title: Hidden function with purpose 'etw disable' at 0x14000194E
findings.description: Function at 0x14000194E performs 'etw disable' but is not reachable via the normal DriverEntry → MajorFunction call graph.  It may be invoked via a timer, DPC, work item, or kernel callback — a hidden secondary attack path.

findings.title: No PreviousMode / RequestorMode check detected
findings.description: Driver does not import ExGetPreviousMode or KeGetPreviousMode and no inline KTHREAD.PreviousMode read was found. Without checking PreviousMode, a kernel-mode caller could be impersonated, or user-mode requests may bypass validation. IOCTLs should verify RequestorMode == UserMode before trusting input.

findings.title: IoCreateDevice without FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN
findings.description: Driver uses IoCreateDevice instead of IoCreateDeviceSecure and FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN flag was not detected. Without this flag, the device object inherits permissive default security, allowing any user to open a handle. Use IoCreateDeviceSecure with an SDDL string to restrict access.

findings.title: No SDDL security descriptor found
findings.description: No SDDL security descriptor string detected in the binary. Drivers should apply SDDL-based ACLs to their device objects to control which users/groups can open handles.

findings.title: Driver has Authenticode signature
findings.description: PE contains a WIN_CERTIFICATE structure with embedded signature.

findings.title: Signed by: NVIDIA Corporation
findings.description:
Subject: NVIDIA Corporation (NVIDIA Corporation)
Issuer: DigiCert Trusted G4 Code Signing RSA4096 SHA384 2021 CA1
Serial: 636afb1da06ca9791388b36e258d048
Key: RSA 3072-bit
Valid until: 2025-10-15T23:59:59+00:00

details.issuer: DigiCert Trusted G4 Code Signing RSA4096 SHA384 2021 CA1

findings.title: Signing certificate EXPIRED (valid until 2025-10-15T23:59:59+00:00)
findings.description: The code-signing certificate has expired. While drivers with a valid timestamp counter-signature remain valid after expiry, an expired cert without a timestamp means the signature is no longer trusted. This is also common in BYOVD — old vulnerable drivers with expired certs that were signed before revocation.

findings.title: No Microsoft certificate in signing chain
findings.description: The certificate chain does not include any Microsoft-issued certificate. Since July 2021, new kernel-mode drivers must be submitted to Microsoft's Hardware Dev Center portal for signing. Drivers without Microsoft in the chain are either legacy (pre-2021), cross-signed with a now-expired cert, or test-signed.

findings.title: No timestamp countersignature detected
findings.description: The Authenticode signature has no countersignature timestamp. Without a timestamp, the signature becomes invalid after the signing certificate expires. Timestamped signatures remain valid indefinitely. This affects driver loadability.

findings.title: PKCS#7 signature verifies cryptographically
findings.description: The primary Authenticode PKCS#7 signature was verified against the signer certificate's public key.

findings.title: Authenticode PE hash matches (binary not tampered)
findings.description: Recomputed sha256 hash of the PE image matches the value embedded in SpcIndirectDataContent.

findings.title: Chain does not terminate at a Windows kernel-trusted root
findings.description: The certificate chain does not chain up to any root present in the Windows kernel trust store (20 known roots checked by thumbprint). Unless a missing intermediate can be supplied by the OS at load time, Windows will not accept this driver.

findings.title: Load may fail: Expired certificate without timestamp
findings.description: The signing certificate has expired and no countersignature timestamp was detected. Without a timestamp proving the driver was signed before expiry, Windows may reject the signature.

findings.title: HVCI incompatible: missing FORCE_INTEGRITY flag
findings.description: This driver is NOT compatible with Hypervisor-protected Code Integrity (HVCI / Memory Integrity). On systems with HVCI enabled, Windows will BLOCK this driver from loading. Issues: missing FORCE_INTEGRITY flag.

findings.title: Driver architecture: x64
findings.description: 64-bit driver — loads on x64 Windows

findings.title: Load compatibility: BLOCKED — 4 issue(s) prevent loading
findings.description:
[BLOCKED] Kernel Trust Anchor: Chain does not terminate at a Windows kernel trust root (no-ms-root)
  [BLOCKED] Expired + No Timestamp: Expired cert without countersignature timestamp
  [BLOCKED] HVCI: Blocked when Memory Integrity is ON (missing FORCE_INTEGRITY flag)
  [PASS] Secure Boot: Compatible (signed driver)
  [BLOCKED] Timestamp: Expired cert + no timestamp = signature rejected

blockers: Chain does not anchor to a kernel-trusted root (no-ms-root)
Chain does not anchor to a kernel-trusted root (no-ms-root)
Chain does not anchor to a kernel-trusted root (no-ms-root)
FORCE_INTEGRITY flag not set (HVCI requires it on the PE header)

passes: Any valid signature accepted under test-signing

blockers: No WHQL attestation EKU — S Mode / Secured-Core require WHQL-signed drivers
No Authenticode page hashes (Secured-Core requires per-page integrity)

findings.title: Device Access Security Audit
findings.description: Uses IoCreateDevice (not IoCreateDeviceSecure); Missing FILE_DEVICE_SECURE_OPEN flag; Device is not exclusive (multiple handles allowed); No SDDL security descriptor — default permissive ACL

findings.title: ETW control imports: EtwUnregister
findings.description: Driver imports ETW control APIs (EtwUnregister). These can disable ETW trace providers that EDR products rely on for kernel telemetry.

findings.title: ROP/JOP gadgets found: 437 total (misc: 161, reg-control: 101, memory-read: 83, memory-write: 65, stack-pivot: 17, jmp-reg: 10)
findings.description:
Found 437 usable ROP/JOP gadgets in executable sections. Since many drivers lack ASLR, these gadgets are at predictable addresses and can be chained for kernel exploitation.

Top gadgets by type:
  [stack-pivot] (17 found):
    0x140001043: pop rsp ; and al, 0x30 ; add rsp, 0x20 ; pop rdi ; ret
    0x140001265: xchg dword ptr [rcx], eax ; add byte ptr [rax], al ; add rsp, 0x48 ; ret
    0x140001263: add al, ch ; xchg dword ptr [rcx], eax ; add byte ptr [rax], al ; add rsp, 0x48 ; ret
  [memory-write] (65 found):
    0x1400011B6: mov dword ptr [rax], ecx ; add rsp, 0x20 ; pop rbx ; ret
    0x1400011B5: mov qword ptr [rax], rcx ; add rsp, 0x20 ; pop rbx ; ret
    0x1400011B2: mov byte ptr [rax + 8], bl ; mov qword ptr [rax], rcx ; add rsp, 0x20 ; pop rbx ; ret
  [memory-read] (83 found):
    0x140001042: mov ebx, dword ptr [rsp + 0x30] ; add rsp, 0x20 ; pop rdi ; ret
    0x140001041: mov rbx, qword ptr [rsp + 0x30] ; add rsp, 0x20 ; pop rdi ; ret
    0x140001040: ret ; mov rbx, qword ptr [rsp + 0x30] ; add rsp, 0x20 ; pop rdi ; ret
  [reg-control] (101 found):
    0x14000104A: pop rdi ; ret
    0x140001047: add esp, 0x20 ; pop rdi ; ret
    0x140001046: add rsp, 0x20 ; pop rdi ; ret
  [jmp-reg] (10 found):
    0x140001F4E: add byte ptr [rax], al ; jmp rax
    0x140001F4C: add byte ptr [rax], al ; add byte ptr [rax], al ; jmp rax
    0x140001F4A: test byte ptr [rax], al ; add byte ptr [rax], al ; add byte ptr [rax], al ; jmp rax
